The surgeon prepares to perform a laparoscopic myomectomy, planning to remove three fibroids: a small 1x1 cm fibroid, a medium 5x5 cm fundal fibroid, and a large 12x12 cm myoma located partly in the stomach and partly on the anterior cervical surface.

This is the patient with the primary infertility. Rasbandhi is aspermic and we are planning for the donor IUI as the tubes were okay. But the NTA fibroid, the cervical fibroid, it is cervical and partly isthmic. It is indenting the endometrium. Hence, we decided to go for a myomectomy first and then after three or four months,

The surgeon uses four laparoscopic ports, two on the left side and one on the right side, for the procedure. A diluted vasopressin solution is injected into the fibroid's stalk, with 20 ml administered from a vial diluted in 100 ml of normal saline.

The surgeon uses a bipolar cutter to address a small subserosal fibroid, noting the absence of major vessels in the area. The fibroid is surrounded by visible circumferential vessels. Vasopressin has been injected to minimize blood loss and maintain a clear surgical field.

The surgeon removes the anterior cervical and isthmic fibroid, which extends into the broad ligament. The posterior view reveals the round ligament, with approximately half of it positioned above the fibroid.

The surgeon separates the bladder peritoneum, which detaches easily, revealing a thin capsule. The myoma is partially embedded in the myometrium and extends into the serosa. Vasopressin is injected into the remaining tissue to minimize blood loss.

The surgeon performs hydro dissection in the area, causing a bulging distal to the puncture site. The tissue gradually blanches, turning to a marble white appearance, indicating effective vasopressin action for bloodless surgery. A transverse elliptical incision is made in the tissue.

The surgeon makes an incision in the center of the myoma, which is larger on the lower side, and positions it slightly upward to avoid the ureter on the lateral edges. The incision is focused on the anterior side for separation and suturing. Vasopressin is observed coming out from between the capsule and the fibroid, revealing the fibroid beneath a very thin capsule.

The surgeon identifies a proper plane for the myomectomy, facilitating easier dissection. The fibroid is excised without using a morcellator, and an endo bag is employed for tissue removal. An incision is extended to approximately three centimeters to extract the fibroids.

The surgeon inserts a myoma screw into the deeply located myoma, with only 20-30% visible. Using a peeling technique, traction is applied to opposite edges for bloodless separation. A ball grasper and harmonic device are used to slowly separate the myoma from surrounding tissue.

The surgeon extends the incision by one or two centimeters to facilitate the myomectomy. The fibroid is gradually being extracted, with separation occurring first on one side and then the other. The myoma screw position is frequently adjusted to maintain effective traction and counter-traction. The fibroid is visibly emerging from the incision.

now after twenty twenty five minutes some bleeder and the oozer will start because of the this vasopression will get washed off many surgeons used to dilute this vasopressin in two hundred ml but as far as my experience is concerned it is better to inject only hundred ml because many a times we don't find this much space between the capsule and the fibroid that can occupy two hundred ml of fluid so it is always better to dilute vasopressin in

The surgeon changes the position of the myoma, applying significant traction to facilitate its inoculation. A barb suture is prepared for closing the myomectomy incision, ensuring the two margins are approximated effectively.

The surgeon identifies the glistening white fibroid, indicating the proper plane of dissection for enucleation. This plane allows for easier removal of the fibroid, despite the complexity of the myomectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate the myoma, with visible gradual oozing as the wash operation effect diminishes. Despite minor oozing, no significant bleeders are encountered during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies multiple fibroids, including a larger fibroid on the anterior aspect and a smaller one on the posterior surface. The pedunculated fibroid is noted, but its removal is deemed unnecessary for infertility treatment as it does not affect the endometrium or sperm passage.

The surgeon applies traction to gradually enucleate the fibroid, which is located more on the right side. The fibroid is slowly emerging as counter traction is applied by an assistant, revealing clearer anatomical structures.

The surgeon focuses on the basal part of the fibroid, where the feeders are located, applying traction and counter traction to aid in enucleation. The myoma screw position is repeatedly adjusted to facilitate removal. A barb suture is used to close the myoma bed and incision site.

The surgeon removes a giant fibroid from the uterus, ensuring the endometrial cavity remains intact by using a uterine sound for manipulation. The fibroid is extracted, leaving only a small stalk attached. The uterus is closed in two layers without opening the full thickness of the cavity.
The surgeon displays a large fibroid resembling a potato, highlighting the feeder vessels that are visible after the myomectomy.

The surgeon observes small bleeders as the effect of vasopressin diminishes. The myometrium is being cut, and the fibroid has been completely removed. Suturing is performed using a barb suture, with the needle introduced through a 5mm port, taking full thickness bites through the muscle layer.
The surgeon takes four suturing bites, securing both the upper and lower margins of the incision site with a barb suture. This technique ensures optimal tension and strength at the suture line.

The surgeon completes the second layer of suturing using a barbed suture, ensuring full thickness bites for optimal uterine strength. The fibroids have been cut into small pieces with scissors. The right side upper secondary port is enlarged to ten millimeters, and the fibroid tissue is removed through an extended three-centimeter incision. The suture margins appear well-aligned, and the urine is clear.
